data.history: The Al 'Uray'ir emerged as a ruling clan within the Bani Khalid tribal confederation, which dominated eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. The Bani Khalid confederation was ruled by various clans, with the Al Humaid traditionally holding chieftainship, but the Al 'Uray'ir specifically held power as governors and rulers of the al-Hasa and al-Qatif regions under the broader Bani Khalid emirate. Under leaders like Barrak ibn Ghurayr, the Bani Khalid expelled Ottoman forces from Eastern Arabia in 1670 and established their capital in Al-Mubarraz. The Al 'Uray'ir's first period of rule came to an end in 1793 when the First Saudi State, led by Abdulaziz bin Muhammad Al Saud, invaded the Bani Khalid domain and deposed them from power. This defeat was part of the broader Saudi-Wahhabi expansion that targeted the Shi'ite-leaning Bani Khalid confederation. When the Ottomans invaded Arabia and overthrew the First Saudi State in 1818, they reinstated members of the Al 'Uray'ir as rulers of the al-Hasa and al-Qatif regions. However, by this time the Bani Khalid were no longer the potent military force they once were, facing encroachment from tribes like the Ajman, Dawasir, Subay', and Mutayr, while being beset by internal leadership disputes. The family's second period of rule was short-lived, as they were eventually defeated by an alliance including the Mutayr, Ajman, Subay', and the re-emergent Al Saud between 1823 and 1830. The Ottomans made one final attempt to install a Bani Khalid governor over al-Hasa in 1874, but this rule was also brief, marking the end of Al 'Uray'ir political power in Eastern Arabia.
data.description: The Al 'Uray'ir family was a ruling clan within the Bani Khalid tribal confederation that governed the al-Hasa and al-Qatif regions of Eastern Arabia during two distinct periods: from the 15th century until 1793, and briefly again from 1818 to 1830. They were part of the broader Bani Khalid confederation that dominated eastern Arabia before being permanently displaced by Saudi-Wahhabi expansion.
data.originStory: The Al 'Uray'ir originated as a ruling clan within the Bani Khalid tribal confederation. The Bani Khalid traditionally claimed descent from Khalid ibn al-Walid, a companion of Muhammad and esteemed general, though this claim has been questioned by Arab genealogists who suggest they may descend from his relatives in Banu Makhzum or alternatively from other Arab tribal origins including Banu Rabi'ah, Banu Ghazia from Tayy, or other lineages.

folkLegends.title: Protectors of the Habari
folkLegends.story: According to Arabian folklore, one chief of the Bani Khalid attempted to protect the prized desert bustard (Habari) from extinction by prohibiting the bedouin in his realm from poaching the bird's eggs, earning the tribe the appellation of 'protectors of the eggs of the Habari', an allusion to the chief's absolute supremacy over his realm.

data.history: The Al Jarba family is a prominent branch of the Shammar tribal confederation, descended from the Tayy tribe which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabia in the second century CE. They emerged as leading figures within the powerful Shammar confederation during the 18th and 19th centuries, establishing authority over vast territories in northern Arabia and the Jazirah region of Mesopotamia. Under the leadership of Al Jarba sheikhs, particularly during the late 18th century conflicts with the first Saudi state, a massive exodus of Shammar occurred into Iraq around 1791-1801. This migration was triggered by Wahabi wars and the killing of Sheikh Muslet bin Motlak Al-Jarba in battles against Ibn Saud. Led by Sheikh Mutlag Aljarba and later his successors including Faris bin Mohammad and Sfoug bin Faris Aljarba, the Al Jarba dominated Shammar migrants in Iraq and became part of a tribal coalition under Ottoman auspices to resist Wahabi raids on southern Iraq from 1798-1801. They successfully established control over the Jazirah region between the Tigris and Euphrates, reaching as far north as Mosul. The Al Jarba maintained their position as Sheikhs of Sheikhs of Shammar, with the family producing notable political figures including Ghazi al-Yawar, who was chosen as interim president of Iraq after the overthrow of Saddam Hussein. The family's influence extended beyond Iraq, with branches settling in Syria where they continue to hold leadership positions among the Shammar there. Notably, King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz Al Saud of Saudi Arabia married multiple women from the Al Jarba family, including Tathi bint Mishan Al Faisal Al Jarba who gave birth to six of his children including Prince Mishaal, Prince Turki, and Princess Oraib, and Malka bint Saud bin Zaid Al Jarba Al Choumri, with whom he had three children: Prince Saad, Princess Sahab and Prince Sultan.
data.description: The Al Jarba family is a prominent branch of the Shammar tribal confederation, historically influential in northern Arabia and Mesopotamia. They have successfully transitioned from traditional tribal leadership to modern political and business prominence across the Arab world, producing high-ranking political figures including an interim president of Iraq.
data.originStory: The Al Jarba emerged as a leading branch within the powerful Shammar tribal confederation during the 18th and 19th centuries. They are descended from the Tayy tribe which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabia in the second century CE, establishing their authority over vast territories in northern Arabia and the Jazirah region of Mesopotamia.
data.modernStatus: The family continues to hold leadership positions among the Shammar in Syria and Iraq, with branches maintaining political influence across the Arab world. They have intermarried with Saudi royalty and continue to be recognized as traditional tribal leaders.

data.history: The Al Qasimi (Qawasim) are a confederation of Sunni tribes that emerged as a dominant maritime power in the Persian Gulf during the 18th century. According to their genealogy, they are Huwala of Hashemite origin, descending from Husayn ibn Ali through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. Their ancestors migrated between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, maintaining rulership over Lengeh on the Hormozgan coast. Around 1727, the Al Qasimi clan took control of Sharjah and declared the polity independent. During the 18th century, the Qawasim embraced the Wahhabi movement and became close allies of the Emirate of Diriyah. By the early 19th century, they controlled territories on both sides of the Persian Gulf, including Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah on the southern shore, and Qishm, Bandar Abbas and Lingeh on the northern shore. Their dominance led to conflicts with the British-allied Omani Empire over trade routes and naval supremacy. Beginning in 1804, increased Qawasim naval attacks on trading ships led the British to label them 'pirates' and mount two major military campaigns against them in 1809 and 1819. The 1820 General Maritime Treaty and subsequent 1853 Perpetual Maritime Truce established British control over the region. Under Sheikh Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi (1781-1866), who ruled from 1803-1866 with interruptions, the Qawasim maintained their confederation structure while adapting to British oversight. The tribe engaged in extensive conflicts with Abu Dhabi in the 1830s and maintained commercial ties reaching the Red Sea and Somali ports of Berbera and Zeila. In 1972, Sheikh Khalid bin Mohammed Al Qasimi was killed during an attempted coup by former ruler Saqr bin Sultan Al Qasimi, leading to Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi's ascension to power. Today, the Al Qasimi remain the only royal family to rule two emirates simultaneously - Sharjah under Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi and Ras Al Khaimah under Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi.
data.description: The Al Qasimi of Sharjah are the ruling family of the Emirate of Sharjah in the UAE, descended from a powerful maritime tribe that controlled Gulf trade routes from the 18th century. Today they are known for their emphasis on culture, education, and heritage preservation under the leadership of Sheikh Sultan bin Muhammad Al Qasimi.
data.originStory: Huwala Arabs of claimed Hashemite descent through Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki, descending from Husayn ibn Ali. Their ancestors migrated between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, maintaining rulership over Lengeh on the Hormozgan coast before establishing control over Sharjah around 1727.
data.modernStatus: Ruling family of Sharjah emirate with extensive cultural and educational initiatives. Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi serves as ruler and is known for historical scholarship. Deputy Ruler Sultan bin Ahmed Al Qasimi chairs multiple government entities and private companies including Basma Group, Tilal Properties, and Arada property development.

data.history: The Al-Buainain are a clan of the Bani Tamim tribe, divided into four main branches: al-Khater, al-Muhammad, al-Ali and Al-Nuwasir. Based primarily in Eastern Province of Saudi Arabia, Qatar, United Arab Emirates and Bahrain, they were historically involved in the pearl diving industry. The Al-Buainain were one of several Bedouin tribes that moved to Bahrain in 1783 after the Al Khalifa conquered the island from Nasr Al-Madhkur. Following dissatisfaction in Qatar, they established the town of Jubail on the Hasa coast in Saudi Arabia and helped build up Al Wakrah in Qatar. According to J.G. Lorimer's Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf, the Al-Buainain population in Al Wakrah was 2,000 in 1908, and the tribe owned around 95 houses in Bahrain, mainly in the towns of Askar and Muharraq. The Al Khater branch of the family became particularly prominent, building major cities including Al-Jubail in Saudi Arabia and Al-Wakrah, the former capital of Qatar. They developed close family and political ties with the ruling families of Saudi Arabia (Al Saud), Bahrain (Al Khalifa), and Qatar (Al Thani) through intermarriage and political alliances. Notable family members have served as rulers of Al Wakrah and Jubail, ministers and ambassadors in Qatar, and maintained significant business interests across the Gulf region.
data.description: A prominent clan of the Bani Tamim tribe with four main branches (al-Khater, al-Muhammad, al-Ali, Al-Nuwasir), based across Saudi Arabia, Qatar, UAE and Bahrain. The Al Khater branch maintains particularly close ties with Gulf ruling families and has produced numerous diplomats, ministers, and city founders.
data.originStory: The Al-Buainain belong to the Bani Tamim tribe, one of the largest Arab tribes that originated in Najd and Hejaz in the Arabian Peninsula. According to traditional genealogy, Bani Tamim descends from Tamim ibn Murr ibn 'Udd ibn Amr ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar bin Nizar bin Ma'add bin Adnan, claimed as a direct lineage to Isma'il bin Ibrahim (Ishmael, son of Abraham).
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant political and business influence across the Gulf. In Qatar, family members have served as ministers, ambassadors, and members of the Consultative Assembly. The Al Khater branch operates businesses including law firms in Bahrain and maintains close relationships with Gulf ruling families through continued intermarriage and political alliances.

data.history:
The Al-Sulaiti family appears to have been among the Arab families that migrated from Qatar to Bahrain following the Bani Utbah conquest in 1783. According to historical records, after the Bani Utbah gained control of Bahrain and the fall of Nasr Al-Madhkur of Bushehr, different Arab family clans and tribes from Qatar moved to Bahrain to settle. The Al-Sulaiti were specifically mentioned among these families, alongside the House of Khalifa, Al-Ma'awdah, Al-Buainain, Al-Fadhil, Al-Kuwari, Al-Mannai, Al-Noaimi, Al-Rumaihi, Al-Sadah, and Al-Thawadi. This suggests they were part of the broader Arab migration from the Qatar peninsula to Bahrain in the late 18th century.

A notable modern figure from the family is Ghanem Al Sulaiti, born in 1956 in Manama, Bahrain, who became a prominent Qatari playwright and actor. His background reflects the transnational nature of Gulf families - his father was Qatari, his mother Emirati, and his grandmother Saudi. He lived in Bahrain during part of his childhood before his family moved to Qatar, illustrating the continued connections between Gulf territories. Ghanem Al Sulaiti graduated from Egypt's Higher Institute of Dramatic Arts in 1978 and went on to become a pioneering figure in Qatari theatre, founding Qatar's first theatrical production company in 1986.

Ghanem Al Sulaiti's theatrical career began in 1971 with his first play and he helped form Qatar's first official theatre group, the Qatari Theatrical Troupe, in 1972. His 1985 play 'The Fighters' (Al Mutarishqun) was one of the most highly regarded plays in Qatari theatre, winning him awards at the Carthage Theatre Festival in Tunisia. He continued to present plays throughout the Persian Gulf region and won the Sharjah Award for Arab Theater Innovation in 2016. His work often addressed political themes, including his controversial 1995 play 'Hello Gulf' which criticized the Gulf Cooperation Council, and his 2018 television series 'What's Happening?' which satirized the Qatar blockade.
data.description: Al-Sulaiti is a Gulf Arab family that migrated from Qatar to Bahrain in the late 18th century following the Bani Utbah conquest. The family maintains connections across the Gulf region, with notable modern contributions to Qatari theatre and arts.
data.originStory: The Al-Sulaiti were among the Arab families that migrated from Qatar to Bahrain following the Bani Utbah conquest in 1783, settling alongside other prominent Arab clans from the Qatar peninsula.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains presence across the Gulf region, with notable contributions to cultural and artistic fields, particularly through Ghanem Al Sulaiti's pioneering work in Qatari theatre and television.

data.history: The Hamdanid dynasty was a Shia Muslim Arab dynasty that ruled northern Mesopotamia and Syria from 890-1004 CE, descended from the ancient Banu Taghlib tribe of Mesopotamia and Arabia. They specifically traced their lineage to Adi ibn Usama al-Taghlibi. The dynasty was founded by Hamdan ibn Hamdun, who established control over Mardin by 892-893 after fighting the Kharijites of the Jazira. The family rose to prominence during the weakening of central Abbasid authority, allowing tribal leaders like Hamdan to establish independent emirates. His sons Husayn and Abdallah expanded Hamdanid control, with Abdallah becoming governor of Mosul in 905-906. The dynasty reached its zenith under two main branches: Hassan Nasir al-Dawla ruled Mosul and Diyar Bakr (929-968), while Ali Sayf al-Dawla 'Sword of the Dynasty' ruled northern Syria from Aleppo (945-967). The Hamdanids became the primary opponents of the Byzantine Empire's re-expansion, with Sayf al-Dawla's court serving as a center of Arabic literary culture. During their rule, the Hamdanids intermarried with Kurdish dignitaries, reflecting their integration with local populations. The dynasty's power waned after a heavy defeat by the Buyids in 979, with their northern Iraq territories eventually divided between the Uqaylids and Marwanids by 990. The Aleppo branch survived longer under Fatimid suzerainty but was finally deposed by the Fatimids in 1003, ending over a century of Hamdanid rule.
data.description: The Hamdanid dynasty was a powerful Arab Shia dynasty that ruled northern Mesopotamia and Syria from 890-1004 CE, renowned for their military campaigns against the Byzantines and patronage of Arabic literature. They originated from the Taghlib tribe and established major centers in Mosul and Aleppo during the fragmentation of Abbasid power.
data.originStory: Founded by Hamdan ibn Hamdun al-Taghlibi, a military commander who served the Abbasid Caliphate and established autonomous rule in Mosul in 890 CE. The dynasty emerged during the weakening of central Abbasid authority, allowing tribal leaders to establish independent emirates.

data.history:
The Uyunid dynasty (Arabic: العيونيون, al-ʿUyūnīyūn) ruled Eastern Arabia from 1077-1253 CE, establishing one of the most stable medieval Arabian states in the region. Founded by Abdullah ibn Ali al-Uyuni around 1077-1078 CE, they seized control from the declining Qarmatian state in al-Ahsa with military assistance from the Great Seljuk Empire during the Siege of Hofuf. Like the majority of Eastern Arabia's population at the time, the Uyunids belonged to the Banu Abdul Qays tribe, an ancient Arabian tribal confederation with deep roots in the region. The dynasty's religious affiliation remains disputed among historians - some sources indicate they were Shia Twelver Muslims, with coins exhibited at the British Museum supporting this claim, while others suggest Sunni Islam, though no reliable sources definitively support the Sunni theory.

The Uyunids controlled the lucrative pearl trade and governed al-Ahsa, Bahrain (then called Awal), Qatif, and surrounding territories including parts of Kuwait and Najd. Their capital moved between Qatif and Awal (modern Bahrain) during different periods of their rule. Al-Fadhl, son of Abdullah, initially transferred the capital to Qatif, then to Awal, extending the state to Kuwait, before the capital returned to Qatif in 513 AH. In 531 AH, Mohammed, son of Al Fadhl I, was assassinated, leading to the state being divided into two parts - one in al-Hasa and another in Qatif.

Under Muhammad b. Ahmad b. Abu'l-Hussin b. Abu Sinan, the Uyunids reached their greatest territorial extent, stretching from Najd to the Syrian desert. Due to their influence, Caliph al-Nasir li-Din Allah granted Muhammad b. Ahmad authority to protect the pilgrimage route to Mecca. However, Muhammad was later murdered by a family member, instigated by his cousin Gharir b. Shukr b. Ali. Between 587-605 AH, Mohammed bin Abi al-Hussain managed to reunite Qatif and Al-Hasa, restoring the dynasty's glory and extending control into central Arabia, but the state was again divided following his assassination in 605 H.

The dynasty finally fell to the Usfurids of Banu Uqayl in 651 AH (1253 CE), marking the end of their 176-year reign over Eastern Arabia. Their downfall came after Uyunid power had been significantly weakened by an invasion in 1235 by the Salghurid Atabeg of Fars. The Uyunid period is remembered as one of the most stable eras in medieval Eastern Arabian history, with their control of maritime trade routes and pearl diving operations forming the economic backbone of their state.
data.description: The Uyunid dynasty was an Arab dynasty from the Banu Abdul Qays tribe that ruled Eastern Arabia from 1077-1253 CE, controlling the pearl trade and governing territories including al-Ahsa, Bahrain, Qatif, and parts of Kuwait and Najd. They established one of the most stable medieval Arabian states before falling to the Usfurids.
data.originStory: Founded by Abdullah ibn Ali al-Uyuni around 1077-1078 CE when he defeated the Qarmatians in Bahrain and al-Hasa during the Siege of Hofuf with military assistance from the Great Seljuk Empire. The dynasty claimed descent from the ancient Banu Abdul Qays tribe of Eastern Arabia.

data.history: The Al Serkal family's historical origins are not definitively established in the available research, though they emerged as a prominent merchant family in the UAE. The family's documented history shows significant involvement in telecommunications and business development, with Nasser bin Abdullatif Alserkal (1917-1990) playing a key role in establishing Dubai State Telephone Company Limited, the UAE's first telecommunication firm. This early involvement in telecommunications positioned the family as pioneers in the UAE's modern infrastructure development. The family's business interests expanded through subsequent generations, with Eisa bin Nasser Alserkal becoming involved in international trade. The family demonstrated notable cultural engagement internationally, gifting the Al-Serkal Mosque to Phnom Penh, Cambodia in 1968, which served as the main mosque in the capital until it was replaced by a new UAE government-gifted building in 2014. The contemporary generation, led by Abdelmonem Bin Eisa Alserkal, has diversified into arts patronage and cultural enterprises while maintaining telecommunications interests. Abdelmonem founded Alserkal Avenue in 2008, transforming a former industrial district in Dubai's Al Quoz area into a significant arts district and cultural hub. The family has successfully transitioned from traditional telecommunications and trade into contemporary cultural enterprises, with Abdelmonem serving on the board of Etisalat Group and receiving international recognition for cultural contributions, including being appointed Chevalier of the French Ordre des Arts et des Lettres in 2023.
data.description: The Al Serkal family is a prominent Emirati merchant family known for their pioneering role in UAE telecommunications and modern involvement in arts patronage and cultural enterprises. They have successfully evolved from traditional business ventures to become significant patrons of contemporary arts while maintaining influential positions in telecommunications and international trade.

data.history:
The Al Dhaheri family descends from the Dhawahir (Arabic: ظَوَاهِر) tribe of the United Arab Emirates, whose main center was the Buraimi Oasis and Al Ain. The tribe originally populated the area of Al Dhahirah in present-day Oman before settling in the Al Ain region. By the early 20th century, the Dhawahir owned much of the cultivated land and property in Al Ain oasis, except for Buraimi village itself. The tribe consisted of three subsections: the Daramikah who populated Hili and Qattara, the Jawabir in Al Ain, and the Bani Saad in Jimi. They maintained large flocks of sheep, herded camels, and traded in charcoal from the fertile woodlands of the oasis, which were irrigated by an effective falaj system.

The Dhawahir established a strong and longstanding alliance with the Al Nahyan ruling family and the Bani Yas confederation. This relationship was formalized in 1840 when Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakbut Al Nahyan took full responsibility for the Dhawahir tribe. The alliance was further strengthened through marriage, as Sheikh Nasser bin Zayed Al Nahyan's mother was Sheikha Amna bint Salah Al Badi from the Bedouin branch of the Dhawahir tribe. However, the relationship was not always smooth - Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan (Zayed the Great) began buying date groves and water rights predominantly from the Dhawahir, changing ownership patterns throughout the oasis and causing the Dhawahir to rebel in 1877. After a month-long conflict, Zayed prevailed and took two Dhawahir Sheikhs hostage. In 1891, he marched on the oasis again and took the Dhawahir's main settlement 'Ain Dhawahir (now Al Ain), building a fort and appointing Ahmad bin Muhammad bin Hilal Al Dhahiri as his headman.

During the political upheavals of the 1920s in Abu Dhabi, the Al Dhaheri played a crucial role in protecting the Al Nahyan family during exile. When Khalifa Al Nahyan orchestrated the 1928 coup against his brother Saqr, three of Sultan's sons - Shakhbut, Hazza, and Zayed - were in exile in Buraimi under the protection of their grandfather's elderly wali: Ahmad bin Muhammad Al-Dhaheri. This protection was instrumental in maintaining the Al Nahyan lineage during the turbulent fratricidal period. Modern Al Dhaheri family members have achieved prominence in various fields, including business, politics, sports, and entertainment, with notable figures like Ali Saeed bin Harmal Al Dhaheri serving as chairman of Abu Dhabi University and film producer Mansoor Al Dhaheri founding Film Gate Productions.
data.description: The Al Dhaheri family is a prominent UAE family descending from the Dhawahir tribe, historically based in Al Ain and the Buraimi Oasis. They have evolved from traditional Bedouin traders and date cultivators to modern business entrepreneurs, government officials, and cultural figures across the UAE.
data.originStory: The Al Dhaheri family descends from the Dhawahir tribe, which originally populated the area of Al Dhahirah in present-day Oman before settling in the Buraimi Oasis and Al Ain region. They established themselves as landowners, traders, and cultivators in the fertile oasis region.
data.modernStatus: Modern Al Dhaheri family members hold prominent positions across various sectors including business (Ali Saeed bin Harmal Al Dhaheri as chairman of Abu Dhabi University), entertainment (film producer Mansoor Al Dhaheri with Film Gate Productions and Al Kalema Productions), sports (racing driver Rashid Al Dhaheri), politics (Jouan Salem Al Dhaheri), and media (writer and journalist Nasser al-Dhaheri).
data.nameEtymology: Al Dhaheri derives from the tribal name Dhawahir (Arabic: ظَوَاهِر), with the singular form being Al Dhahiri (Arabic: ٱلظَّاهِرِي). The name is connected to their original area of Al Dhahirah in present-day Oman.

data.history: The research reveals multiple distinct individuals with the Al Mana surname but establishes no clear genealogical connection to the existing Qatar-based merchant Al Mana family data from the Tamim tribe. The most prominent figure identified is Aisha al-Mana, a Saudi activist and businesswoman born in 1948 in Khobar. She works as director of Al-Mana General Hospitals and board member of Ebrahim M. Almana and Brothers, suggesting a separate Saudi business dynasty. Aisha became one of the first Saudi women to earn a PhD in 1982 from the University of Colorado Boulder, established the first all-women company in Saudi Arabia (Al-Khalijiah Development Company) in 1985, and became the first female hospital director in the kingdom in 1990. She participated in three driving ban protest campaigns (1990, 2011, 2013) and established educational foundations supporting Saudi women's health education. The research also identifies several Al Hajri family members with Mana as a given name, including Mana bin Abdul Hadi Al Hajri (former Qatari ambassador to Jordan) and his children Al-Anoud (second wife of Emir Tamim) and Abdulhadi (businessman who purchased London's Ritz Hotel in 2020). However, these Al Hajri family members represent a distinct lineage with no established connection to any Al Mana merchant family. The lack of direct genealogical evidence linking these various Mana-named individuals suggests either separate family lines or insufficient documentation of connections to the original Qatari Tamim-descended Al Mana merchant family described in the existing data.

data.history: The Al Shafar family emerges in the research as a prominent Emirati construction and contracting dynasty, though their specific tribal or ethnic origins remain unclear from available sources. The family's business empire was established through a partnership between a Dubai national and an Egyptian engineer, suggesting possible mixed origins. The business was formally established in 1989 with the founding of ASGC (Al Shafar General Contracting Co. LLC) as part of the larger Al Shafar Group, which has grown to encompass 26 companies. The family gained significant prominence in Dubai's construction sector, being awarded major contracts including construction work for Al Furjan development by Nakheel in 2008. When Dubai's construction sector faced severe economic pressures in 2009 and many projects were halted, Al Shafar demonstrated remarkable resilience by being the first contractor to restart work, highlighting their strong market position and financial stability. Under the leadership of Mohamed Saif Bin Shafar as chairman, the company expanded dramatically, achieving annual revenues of $1 billion by 2020 with 17,000 employees. ASGC has been responsible for constructing high-profile Dubai landmarks including the Ministry of Public Works building, Dubai Police headquarters, sectors of Jumeirah Beach Residence, and the Mohammad Bin Rashid Library. The family has also pursued international expansion, notably becoming a major shareholder in the UK's Costain Group in 2020, though they later sold this stake in 2024. By 2021-2022, new leadership under Ahmad Bin Shafar helped revitalize the company's performance and fan support.
data.description: The Al Shafar family is a prominent Emirati construction dynasty that owns ASGC Construction and the broader Al Shafar Group comprising 26 companies. They are known for major Dubai infrastructure projects and achieved $1 billion in annual revenues by 2020 with 17,000 employees.
data.originStory: Origins unclear from available research. The business was established through a partnership between a Dubai national and an Egyptian engineer, suggesting possible local Emirati origins for the Al Shafar family component, though their specific tribal or ethnic background is not detailed in the sources.

Led by Mohamed Saif Bin Shafar (chairman) and currently under new administration led by Ahmad Bin Shafar. Major contractor for Dubai government and private projects.

data.history: The Bugshan family is a prominent Hadhrami merchant family that established themselves in Khailah within the Wadi Dawan branch of the Hadhramaut region of Yemen. They built distinctive palaces notable for their elaborate oil-based painted decorations on exteriors, distinguishing them architecturally from other structures in the area. This architectural legacy serves as a notable example of affluent Hadhrami construction and indicates the family's significant wealth and influence in the Hadhramaut region. The family appears to have accumulated substantial wealth through traditional Hadhrami trading networks, following the pattern of other successful Hadhrami merchant families who built fortunes through commerce across the Indian Ocean region. In modern times, the Bugshan family has evolved into a significant business conglomerate with international reach. Their modern prominence is evidenced by their status as clients of high-profile international legal services, including the law firm of former French Prime Minister Dominique de Villepin, indicating their continued significance in global business circles. The family represents the successful transition of traditional Hadhrami merchant wealth into contemporary multinational business operations.

data.history: The Al Jomaih family has emerged as a prominent Saudi Arabian business dynasty with significant international investments spanning technology and energy sectors. Their business activities gained international recognition through Al-Jomaih Investment Group, which became one of the main shareholders in Crossgate AG, a German B2B integration company founded in 2001. Crossgate, which developed network approaches to B2B transactions, attracted strategic investment from SAP SE in 2008, with Al-Jomaih Investment Group joining SAP founder Dietmar Hopp's family and Otto Wolff von Amerongen as the company's main shareholders. The family's investment proved highly successful when SAP acquired Crossgate for a three-digit million amount in September 2011. In the energy sector, the Al Jomaih family established Al-Jomaih Power Limited as their energy investment vehicle, which became a key component of KES Power, a joint venture that acquired majority control of Pakistan's K-Electric Limited in 2005. Through KES Power - a joint venture with National Industries Group of Kuwait and the Infrastructure and Growth Capital Fund (IGCF) - Al-Jomaih Power holds significant influence over Pakistan's only vertically integrated utility company, which serves 3.7 million customers across Karachi and surrounding regions in Sindh and parts of Balochistan. The family's diversified investment strategy demonstrates their evolution from regional business interests to major international players, with successful exits in European technology companies and ongoing strategic control of critical infrastructure assets in South Asia.
data.description: Saudi Arabian business family known for major international investments through Al-Jomaih Investment Group and Al-Jomaih Power Limited, with significant stakes in technology companies like Crossgate AG (acquired by SAP in 2011) and controlling interest in Pakistan's K-Electric Limited through the KES Power joint venture.

data.history: The Behbehani family originates from the Iranian city of Behbahan in Khuzestan province, with Marafi Behbehani documented as one of the first merchants to settle in Kuwait in the 18th century. Behbahan itself has ancient roots dating back to the Elamite era and was historically known as Arrajan, which was rebuilt by Sasanian King Kavadh I in the early 6th century using deported prisoners from Roman territories. The Behbehani family became significant players in Kuwait's early commercial development, with the arms trade being exclusively under the domain of Ajam (Persian) merchants like the Behbahanis. The family made substantial contributions to Kuwait's modernization efforts - Yusuf Behbehani built the first hotel in Kuwait City, while Murad Behbehani was the first person to officially introduce television to Kuwait and founded Kuwait Television (KTV) before it was nationalized by the government. By the 1950s, approximately 40-50,000 Persian immigrants, including the Behbehani family, adopted Kuwaiti citizenship to maintain their economic stronghold in the country. The Behbahanis, along with the Marafi family, maintained substantial economic influence through this period. The family is part of Kuwait's Ajam community, which historically resided in the Sharq district of old Kuwait City and communicated in Persian, forming a linguistic enclave that preserved Kuwaiti Persian for generations until oil-led urbanization scattered them to the suburbs. There are also connections to religious scholarship, as documented in the al-Shahristani family genealogy where Muhammad-Husayn al-Shahristani married the granddaughter of al-Wahid al-Behbehani through his son Muhammad-Ali al-Kirmanshahi al-Behbehani, indicating clerical scholarly networks extending from Iran to Iraq and the Gulf.
data.description: A prominent Persian merchant family from Behbahan, Iran, who were among the first to settle in Kuwait in the 18th century and played key roles in the country's early commercial development, television introduction, and modernization efforts.
data.originStory: The family originates from the Iranian city of Behbahan in Khuzestan province, with Marafi Behbehani being documented as one of the first merchants to settle in Kuwait in the 18th century.
data.modernStatus: Part of Kuwait's established Ajam community with historical business influence, though specific current business holdings not detailed in available research.
data.nameEtymology: The family name derives from Behbahan, their Iranian city of origin. Behbahan itself may mean 'good palace/house surrounded by gardens' (beh = good, bahan = palace/big house with orchards) or alternatively 'better than tents' referring to reconstruction after earthquakes destroyed the ancient city of Arrajan.

data.history: The Al Jaidah family appears prominently in Qatar's cultural history of the 1950s, with Mohammed Al-Jaida emerging as one of the significant artists who helped establish Al Jasrah district as the hub of Qatari cultural life during this period. Al-Jaida was part of a notable group of artists including Jassim Zaini, Yousef Ahmad, Hassan Al Mulla, Ali Hassan, and Salman Al-Malik who gathered in the Al Jasrah district of Doha. Their shared passion for art and childhood stories led to the establishment of Qatar's first art group called 'The Three Friends' and later contributed to the founding of the Qatari Society for Fine Arts. The family also had commercial connections to Doha's historic market areas, as evidenced by the existence of 'Souq Jaidah' in the Al Jasrah district, suggesting they were involved in the traditional souq trade that was central to Qatar's pre-oil economy. However, specific details about the family's tribal origins, migration patterns, or broader business activities beyond these cultural and commercial references are not documented in the available historical records.
data.description: A Qatari family historically associated with the arts and culture scene in 1950s Doha, with commercial connections to the traditional souq areas of the Al Jasrah district.

data.history: The Bahwan family is a prominent Omani business dynasty that has built one of the country's most successful diversified conglomerates. The family's business empire is led by Suhail Bahwan (1939-2025), who passed away in 2025 at age 86 after decades of building the family's commercial interests. The research does not provide specific details about the family's ethnic origins, tribal affiliations, or early settlement patterns in Oman, making their historical roots uncertain. The Bahwan business operations have become particularly notable in the technology sector through Bahwan CyberTek Group, a global software company that has served as a launching pad for international tech entrepreneurs. The company has maintained significant operations in India, with offices in locations like Thoraipakkam, Chennai, where it is listed among the major IT/ITES companies in the area. Bahwan CyberTek has been instrumental in developing tech talent, with notable figures like V.M. Muralidharan serving as Chief Operating Officer since 2010, and Jay Vijayan beginning his career there before moving to Singapore and the United States to eventually found the multi-billion dollar company Tekion Corp. The family's business model appears to focus on technology services and software development, with their company serving as a bridge between Middle Eastern business interests and global tech markets. Additionally, there are indications of involvement in the hospitality sector, with architect John Elliott having worked on a 'Bahwan Resort Master Plan' in Muscat, Oman, suggesting the family's diversified business interests extend beyond technology into tourism and real estate development.
data.description: Prominent Omani business family known for building a diversified conglomerate empire, particularly in technology through Bahwan CyberTek Group, a global software company that has launched several successful tech entrepreneurs.

data.history:
The Adwan tribe traces its origins to the late 16th/early 17th century when two brothers, Fayez and Fauzan of the Al Dhafir tribe (descendants of Tayy), sought refuge with the Al Kinda tribe near Jebel Samik around 1640. After Fauzan's early death, Fayez integrated into local tribal hierarchy through marriage to the daughter of the Sheikh of Kinda. Their son Adwan forged a strategic alliance through marriage with a daughter of the Mihdawiya sheikhs, establishing the foundation for the tribal confederation that would bear his name. This genealogical account shows the Adwan as a relatively recent tribal formation emerging from the ancient Dhafir confederation.

Hamdan, Adwan's son, became a pivotal figure in establishing tribal power through a successful raid on a caravan traveling from northern Iraq to Mecca at Al Zarqa stream, acquiring a she-camel named 'Al Zabta' that became a war cry for the tribe. Over two decades of tensions with the Mihdawiya Emir Jaudat culminated in revolt when Hamdan's followers, known as Al Qradha ('The Borrowed'), supported his resistance against oppressive demands. Following Hamdan's death, his sons continued the struggle, ultimately killing Jaudat in an ambush at Al Fheis around 1750. Under Adwan bin Hamdan's leadership, the tribe consolidated control over the Balqa region, driving the remnant Mihdawiya forces north beyond Beisan into the Ajlun District.

The tribe faced significant challenges from regional powers, including Zahir Al Omar who captured Al-Salt in 1760, forcing temporary retreat to Al-Karak. Leadership struggles between brothers Diyab and Salih marked the late 18th century, with Salih's execution leading to temporary exile in Jabal Al Druze before returning under the famous poet Nimr Al-Adwan (1735-1823). The 19th century saw the Adwan form strategic alliances, notably the 1860s marriage alliance between Ali ibn Diyab Al Adwan's sister Alia and Sattam Al-Fayez of the Bani Sakher, ending centuries of hostility between the tribes.

This Adwan-Bani Sakher alliance posed sufficient threat to Ottoman authority that multiple military campaigns were launched against Adwan lands in 1867 and 1869, resulting in heavy fines and the installation of Ottoman governors. The establishment of Circassian settlements in Amman significantly impacted Adwan prestige and territorial control, marking the beginning of their decline as a regional power in the late Ottoman period. The Ta'amireh tribe was also involved in conflicts with the Adwan, including a 1853 intervention where the Ta'amireh and Tiyaha Bedouins supported rival Adwan leaders Nimmr and Abdulaziz during a period of weakened Ottoman control.

data.history:
Al Araytham is documented as one of the sections within the Albu Salih tribal confederation, which emerged as a powerful alliance in 18th century Iraq. The Albu Salih originated from the Bani Malik of the Al-Muntafiq confederation and transformed into a tribal confederation due to their opposition to Ottoman Empire influence in the region. Under the leadership of Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh (1845-1942), the confederation became the most influential tribe in the Bani Malik region of the Muntafiq Emirate. Al Araytham appears in the documented list of the main sections of Albu Salih, alongside other sections including Al Rumaiyadh (from Bani Malik), Al Nasrallah (from Shammar), Al Khlawi (from Bani A'ridh), Al Omar (from Rabe'a), Al Yosha'a (from Bani Hashem), Al Tarayma, Al Kurdi, Al Zrgan, Al Rahal, Al Safafaa, Al Shamla, Al Shahr, and Al Qoba.

Historically, the confederation consisted of both settled villagers near the great marshes of southern Iraq and semi-nomadic branches who migrated seasonally. The nomadic branches, including Al Araytham members, would migrate south into the desert annually during autumn, moving through the Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait and reaching the Arabian Gulf coast, where they traded with local villages and towns before returning north for summer. This seasonal migration pattern was a defining characteristic of their traditional lifestyle, connecting Iraqi tribal territories with the Arabian Peninsula.

Al Araytham fought alongside other Albu Salih sections in the Battle of Shaiba in 1915 against British forces, supporting the Ottoman Empire despite the confederation's historical rivalry with the Ottomans. This participation was motivated by the Fatwa of Jihad released by religious scholars in 1914 against foreign British forces. During the 1940s and early 1950s, the majority of nomadic members, including those from Al Araytham, settled permanently in various locations, abandoning their traditional seasonal migration patterns.

Today, members of the broader Albu Salih confederation, including Al Araytham, are located across Iraq, Kuwait, Iran, and Saudi Arabia. In Iraq, they are concentrated in Al Islah in Dhi Qar province, and can also be found in Amarah, Basra (Safwan and Zubayr), and Al Diwaniyah. The current leader of the Albu Salih confederation is Shaikh Sabah bin Fares bin Mohsen bin Bader Al Rumaiyadh, continuing the leadership lineage established by the historic Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh.

data.history: The Al Bu Hamir (Arabic: آل بو حمير) is a small tribal section within the Bani Yas confederation, originating from the Al Ain region of Abu Dhabi emirate in the United Arab Emirates. The singular form is Al-Hamiri, also spelled Al-Humairi or Al-Hemeiri. Within the hierarchical structure of the Bani Yas, they were classified as a subsection of the Manasir tribe. The Bani Yas confederation itself originated from the Najd region of Central Arabia, descending from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a, and settled in the Liwa oasis around 1700. The Al Bu Hamir followed Maliki Sunni Islam and politically aligned with the Hinawi faction in Arabian Peninsula politics. Around the turn of the 20th century, the Al Bu Hamir comprised approximately 60 houses in Abu Dhabi, where they had settled from their original Al Ain homeland. Members of the Al Bu Hamir maintained notably close relationships with the Al Nahyan ruling family throughout their history, with several tribe members holding important positions in Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan's administration and royal court. However, the tribe appears to have experienced demographic challenges during the mid-20th century. They were among the sections recorded by J.C. Lorimer in his early 20th century Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf, but were not subsequently mentioned in later comprehensive tribal surveys by J.B. Kelly or in the United Kingdom Memorial on the Buraimi dispute, suggesting possible integration into other sections or migration during the period when many Bani Yas families moved to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia seeking employment opportunities in the oil and construction sectors.

data.history:
Al Bu Hemair (الحميري) is documented as one of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation in the United Arab Emirates. The research explicitly lists Al Hemairi/Al Bu Hemair (آل بو حمير/الحميري) among the branches that formed part of the Bani Yas confederation at various times throughout history. The Bani Yas tribe originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia, descending from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a, and the confederation settled in the Liwa oasis around 1700 under the leadership of the Al Bu Falah clan.

As part of the broader Bani Yas confederation, Al Bu Hemair would have participated in the tribal movements and settlements that characterized the confederation's history. The Bani Yas initially established themselves in the Liwa oasis around 1700, later expanding to Al Ain and Buraimi when the Al Bu Falah clan acquired property in the Buraimi Oasis. In 1793-1795, a significant migration occurred when the Al Bu Falah moved to Abu Dhabi island following the discovery of fresh water there, establishing the town of Abu Dhabi which became the seat of the emirate.

Historical documentation of Al Bu Hemair shows some inconsistencies in tribal records over time. J.C. Lorimer identified Al Bu Hamir in his 1907 documentation of the Bani Yas confederation, listing them among 15 sections he recorded. However, later observers like J.B. Kelly did not subsequently mention this branch, and Al Bu Hamir was among several sections (including Al Falah, Qanaisat, Qasal, Bani Shikir, and Al Sultan) that were recorded by Lorimer but not mentioned in later sources or the UK Memorial on the Buraimi dispute. This absence from later documentation suggests possible changes in tribal organization, nomenclature, or integration patterns within the confederation over the course of the 20th century.

The Bani Yas confederation as a whole experienced significant demographic changes throughout the 20th century. From Lorimer's estimated population of around 12,000 in the early 1900s, the numbers declined to approximately 8,000 by the early 1950s, largely due to emigration to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia in search of employment in oil and construction sectors. The 1968 Abu Dhabi census recorded 5,884 Bani Yas members within the shaikhdom. As a branch of this confederation, Al Bu Hemair would have been subject to these same demographic pressures and population movements that characterized Gulf society during the pre-oil and early oil eras.

data.history: Al Khlawi is documented as one of the main sections of the Albu Salih tribal confederation, with origins tracing to the Bani A'ridh lineage. The Albu Salih confederation emerged during the 18th century as a warfare alliance opposing Ottoman Empire influence in the region, transforming from the original Bani Malik of the Al-Muntafiq. Under the leadership of Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh (1845-1942), the confederation became the most influential tribe in the Bani Malik region of the Muntafiq Emirate. Al Khlawi, as part of this confederation, participated in the complex tribal dynamics of southern Iraq and northern Arabia. The Albu Salih territory was centered around the great marshes in what is now the district of Al Islah in Dhi Qar province, established in 1918 by Shaikh Sulaiman bin Mansur Al Nasrallah. The confederation included both settled agricultural communities near the marshes and semi-nomadic branches who migrated seasonally between the marshlands of southern Iraq and the northeastern Arabian desert, moving through the Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait and reaching the Arabian Gulf coast for trade. During the early 20th century, Al Khlawi members, alongside other Albu Salih sections, were involved in resistance against British colonial forces. They participated in the 1915 Battle of Shaiba, fighting alongside the Ottomans and Muntafiq under the Al Sadun family leadership to retake Basrah from the British, despite their traditional rivalry with the Ottomans. The tribe also played a role in the 1920 Revolution against British rule. By the 1940s and early 1950s, most nomadic members had abandoned the nomadic lifestyle and settled permanently across Iraq, Kuwait, Iran, and Saudi Arabia, maintaining their tribal identity within the modern state system while preserving connections to their ancestral territories in the marshlands of southern Iraq.

data.history:
Al-Nammir ibn Qasit was a pre-Islamic Arabian tribe belonging to the Rabi'ah branch of the Adnanite tribal confederation. According to the research, they are explicitly listed as one of the sub-tribes under Bakr ibn Wa'il in eastern Najd, indicating their position within the broader Rabi'ah genealogical structure. The tribe's placement under the Bakr ibn Wa'il confederation suggests they shared the general territorial and cultural patterns of this major tribal group.

Geographically, al-Nammir was located in eastern Najd, as confirmed by their listing among the eastern Najd tribes alongside Taghlib ibn Wa'il and Anz ibn Wa'il. This positioning placed them within the sphere of influence of the larger Bakr ibn Wa'il confederation, which according to the research had its origins in Najd before many of its sections migrated northward to Upper Mesopotamia after their victory at the Battle of Dhi Qar against the Sasanian Empire.

As part of the Bakr ibn Wa'il tribal structure, al-Nammir would have been connected to a confederation known for its military prowess and involvement in major pre-Islamic conflicts. The Bakr ibn Wa'il tribes were noted for their role in significant battles, their control of trade routes between Hejaz and Mesopotamia, and their eventual conversion to Islam during the early Islamic period. However, the specific historical activities and fate of al-Nammir ibn Qasit as a distinct sub-tribal unit are not detailed in the available research.

The tribal genealogy places al-Nammir within the Adnanite lineage through the Rabi'ah branch, specifically under Bakr ibn Wa'il, making them part of one of the most ancient and prominent Arab tribal confederations. Their inclusion in the eastern Najd tribal listings suggests they maintained their identity as a recognized sub-tribal unit within this larger confederation structure during the pre-Islamic period.

The research provided does not contain any direct information about the Al-Rabbab tribe specifically. The existing data claims Al-Rabbab is one of the four main branches of Banu Tamim, but this assertion cannot be verified against the research materials provided, which focus solely on the genealogical lineage from Prophet Muhammad to Adnan and general information about Adnanite tribal confederations. The research confirms that Adnanite tribes trace their lineage back to Ishmael through Adnan, and that they originated from the Hejaz region as part of the broader Ishmaelite Arab tribal confederation. However, without specific documentation about Al-Rabbab in the research materials, no verified historical narrative about this particular tribe can be constructed. The research does establish that Adnanite tribes are considered the Northern Arabs, distinct from the Qahtanite Arabs of southern Arabia, and that some modern historians question the traditional distinction between these groups, suggesting it may have developed during later Umayyad factional conflicts.

Al Tarayma (الترايمة) is one of the main sections within the Albu Salih tribal confederation, which emerged as a powerful alliance in the Arabian Peninsula during the 18th century. The Albu Salih confederation originated from the Bani Malik of the Al-Muntafiq and transformed into a warfare alliance under the leadership of Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh due to opposition against Ottoman Empire influence in the region. As part of this confederation, Al Tarayma participated in the traditional lifestyle that combined settled agriculture near the great marshes of southern Iraq with seasonal nomadic migrations.

Historically, the nomadic branches of Albu Salih, including Al Tarayma, would migrate south into the northeastern parts of Arabia during autumn. They moved through the Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait, reaching the Arabian Gulf coast where they traded with local villages and towns before returning north for summer. The Albu Salih clans were known to be well-armed during these migrations, and they typically traveled alongside other Muntafiq tribes such as the Shuraifat, Juwarin, and Al Aunan.

During the early 20th century, Al Tarayma, as part of the Albu Salih confederation, were involved in resistance against British forces during World War I. They participated in the Battle of Shaiba in 1915 alongside Ottoman forces, fighting under the leadership of the Al Sadun family (Emirs of the Muntafiq) in an attempt to retake Basrah from the British. This participation was motivated by the Fatwa of Jihad released by religious scholars in 1914 against foreign British forces.

By the 1940s and early 1950s, most nomadic sections of the Albu Salih, including Al Tarayma, had abandoned the nomadic lifestyle and settled in various locations. Today, members of the broader Albu Salih confederation are located across Iraq, Kuwait, Iran, and Saudi Arabia. In Iraq, they are concentrated in Al Islah district in Dhi Qar province, with populations also found in Amarah, Basra (Safwan and Zubayr), and Al Diwaniyah.

data.history: The research provided contains no information about the Almadhakir tribe. The available research focuses exclusively on the House of Maktoum (Āl Maktūm), the ruling family of Dubai since 1833, which is entirely unrelated to the Almadhakir tribal entity. The House of Maktoum is documented as a branch of the Bani Yas clan that established control over Dubai in 1833 under Sheikh Maktoum bin Butti's leadership, but this provides no genealogical, historical, or territorial information about Almadhakir. Without research materials specifically addressing the Almadhakir tribe, their origins, lineage affiliations, historical migrations, territorial holdings, or role in Arabian Peninsula tribal structures cannot be verified or documented.

The Banu Amir ibn Sa'sa'a was one of the largest and most influential tribal confederations in pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia, descended from the Hawazin group and tracing their lineage to Adnan. Their original homeland was the border area between Najd and Hejaz in Khurmah and Ranyah. The confederation consisted of several major branches: Banu Kilab (descendants of Kilab ibn Rabi'ah ibn 'Amir ibn Sa'sa'a), who were Bedouins living in western Nejd and led the confederation prior to Islam; Banu Numayr, who lived on the western borders of al-Yamamah; and Banu Ka'b, the largest section divided into Banu Uqayl, Banu Ja'dah, Banu Qushayr, and Al Harish. The Banu Hilal, a major sub-tribe, originated in Najd and sometimes travelled towards Iraq in search of pastures and oases, with their full genealogy being Hilāl ibn ʿĀmir ibn Ṣaʿṣaʿa ibn Muʿāwiya ibn Bakr ibn Hawāzin ibn Manṣūr ibn ʿIkrima ibn K̲h̲aṣafa ibn Qays ibn ʿAylān ibn Muḍar ibn Nizār ibn Ma'ad ibn ʿAdnān.

The tribe engaged in the Fijar War against the Quraysh before Islam and gave late allegiance to Muhammad. During Muhammad's era, the tribe was involved in military conflicts, including the incident where Abu Bara guaranteed safety for Muslim missionaries to Najd, leading to the tragic Bi'r Ma'una massacre where multiple Banu Amir sub-tribes, including Banu Kilab, were involved in conflicts with early Muslim forces. The Banu Hilal likely did not accept Islam until after Muhammad's victory at the Battle of Hunayn in 630, but like other ʿĀmirid tribes, they did not join in the Ridda Wars that followed Muhammad's death in 632. The confederation produced notable poets including Labid ibn Rabi'ah (author of one of the Seven Hanged Poems) and the protagonists of Layla wal Majnun saga.

After Islam's rise, various branches migrated extensively across the Islamic world. Banu Kilab later moved north to Syria and established significant political power, with their leader Zufar ibn al-Harith leading Qaysiya resistance against the Umayyad Caliphate until making peace in 691. Major migrations occurred in the 9th and 10th centuries, culminating in Banu Kilab's dominance of northern Syria and establishment of the Mirdasid dynasty centered in Aleppo from 1025-1080 under Salih ibn Mirdas. The Banu Hilal underwent massive westward migrations to the Maghreb in the 11th century when the Fatimid Caliphate dispatched them against the Zirid dynasty, with an estimated 200,000 families moving westward and fundamentally transforming the cultural and linguistic landscape of North Africa through Arabization.

Banu Numayr left for Iraq after 9th-century Abbasid campaigns, while Banu Uqayl established the Uqaylid dynasty in Mosul and later returned to Arabia to found the Usfurid and Jabrid dynasties in Bahrain province. The Jabrids, as the ruling class of Bani Khalid tribe, established Arab rule over Eastern Arabia and Nejd from the 15th to 16th century, making Al-Ahsa their capital. Modern tribal connections include the Bani Yas of the UAE and Bani Amer of UAE/Oman tracing descent to Yas bin Amer bin Sa'sa'ah and Amer bin Rabia bin Amer bin Sa'sa'ah respectively, while tribes including Subay', Suhool in Nejd, and sections of Bani Khalid also trace lineage to this ancient confederation, demonstrating the enduring influence of Amir ibn Sa'sa'a across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond.

folkLegends.title: The Protector of the Habari Eggs

data.history:
The Aws and Khazraj were two major Arab tribes of Qahtanite origin who migrated from Yemen and settled in Yathrib (later Medina) in the pre-Islamic period. According to traditional genealogical sources, both tribes descended from the larger Azd confederation that had migrated northward from Yemen, likely following disruptions to the agricultural and political systems there. The tribes established themselves as the dominant Arab population in the Medina oasis, where they coexisted alongside three major Jewish tribes: Banu Qaynuqa, Banu Nadir, and Banu Qurayza, as well as smaller Jewish clans.

For nearly a century before the arrival of Islam, the Aws and Khazraj were locked in bitter rivalry and periodic warfare, with each tribe seeking to gain dominance over the oasis and its resources. This conflict was complicated by their relationships with the Jewish tribes, who were often drawn into the disputes as allies of one side or the other. The rivalry reached its climax in the Battle of Bu'ath around 617 CE, where the two tribes fought a decisive engagement that left both sides exhausted and their leadership depleted.

The arrival of Islam fundamentally transformed the relationship between the two tribes. In 620 CE, six members of the Khazraj encountered Muhammad during a pilgrimage to Mecca and converted to Islam. This initial conversion spread throughout both tribes, facilitated by the preaching of Mus'ab ibn Umayr, who had been sent to Medina by Muhammad. By 622 CE, a substantial number from both tribes had embraced Islam and pledged their support to Muhammad in what became known as the pledges of Aqaba. Notable early converts included Sa'd ibn Mu'adh from the Aws, whose conversion reportedly led to the immediate conversion of his entire Abdul-Ashhal clan.

When Muhammad and his followers migrated to Medina in 622 CE during the Hijra, the Aws and Khazraj collectively became known as the Ansar (the Helpers), welcoming and supporting the Meccan Muslims (Muhajirun). They were formally incorporated into the Constitution of Medina as 'one nation with the Believers,' ending their centuries-old rivalry in favor of Islamic unity. Both tribes played crucial roles in the early military campaigns of Islam, including the battles of Badr, Uhud, and the Trench. Sa'd ibn Mu'adh of the Aws became particularly prominent, serving as the judge who determined the fate of the Jewish Banu Qurayza tribe after their siege, before dying from wounds sustained in the Battle of the Trench. The descendants of both tribes maintained their significance in Islamic history, with some branches establishing lasting legacies including custodial roles in Jerusalem and claims to later dynastic foundations in Al-Andalus.
No research data was provided for the Bani Ham Mazyud tribe. Without verifiable sources, it would be inappropriate and potentially harmful to provide historical claims about this tribal group.

Bani Rasheed (Rashaida) are descendants of Rasheed Al-Zaul Al-Absi, a renowned 7th-century warrior from the ancient Banu Abs tribe. The Banu Abs were a powerful Bedouin confederation that formed a branch of the Ghatafan tribal alliance, inhabiting the outskirts of Medina in an area now called Hurrat Bani Rasheed. Their genealogical line traces through Abs ibn Baghid ibn Rayth ibn Ghatafan ibn Sa'd ibn Qays ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, making them Northern Adnanite Arabs descended from Ishmael through Adnan.

Historically, the Banu Abs were known for their independence and bravery, being called one of the Jamarat (most powerful tribes) of the Arabs who took allegiance to no one but themselves. The tribe was famous for the 40-year war of al-Dahhas wal Ghabra against their brother tribe Banu Dhubyan, and their traditions recall 3,000 of them repelling a Sasanian attack of 20,000. During the early Islamic period, some Absis like Antarah ibn Shaddad became legendary warrior-poets, while others served as companions to Prophet Muhammad and later held important positions in the Umayyad and Abbasid administrations.

The Banu Abs gained significant influence during the Umayyad period through the marriage of Caliph Abd al-Malik to the Absi noblewoman Wallada, who mothered future caliphs al-Walid I and Sulayman. This connection brought the tribe substantial estates in Syria, particularly around Manbij and Damascus, where they established the town of Hiyar Bani Qa'qa. The tribe remained politically active through the Abbasid period, with members serving as governors and military commanders. As late as the 10th and 11th centuries, the Banu Abs continued to occupy their Syrian territories while maintaining tribal structure and customs despite becoming largely sedentarized.

In 1846, as the First Saudi State consolidated power in Arabia, many Rashaida fled across the Red Sea to settle in the coastal plains from Port Sudan to Massawa, Eritrea. They maintained their traditional Bedouin culture, dress, customs, and Sunni Islamic practices while becoming primarily nomadic pastoralists specializing in camel breeding. The Rashaida populations eventually spread to Kuwait, Sudan, Eritrea, Jordan, Yemen, UAE, Oman, and other regions. A 2022 genetic study confirmed their Arabian origins, showing Sudanese Rashaida Arabs have 95.1% Middle Eastern ancestry, higher than populations from Yemen or Lebanon, confirming their lack of genetic mixing with neighboring African groups. Today, significant populations can be found across the Arabian Peninsula, particularly in Saudi Arabia where they maintain strong genealogical traditions and social cohesion, as well as in their diaspora communities that preserve clan-based traditions including tribal leadership structures and family honor codes.

folkLegends.title: The Prophet Khalid ibn Sinan
folkLegends.story: Absi traditions tell of their prophet Khalid ibn Sinan who taught them Biblical monotheism and the worship of God as 'al-ahad al-samad' prior to Muhammad. It was told that Khalid saved his tribe and that most men of the Abs rejected this prophet at the time. Some Muslims have claimed that Khalid prophesied the 'last' prophet after him, who in Islam is Muhammad.

folkLegends.title: Repelling the Sasanian Army
folkLegends.story: Their traditions recall 3,000 warriors from the Banu Abs successfully repelling a massive Sasanian attack of 20,000 soldiers, demonstrating their legendary military prowess despite being vastly outnumbered.

data.nameEtymology: The name 'Bani Rasheed' derives from their ancestor Rasheed Al-Zaul Al-Absi, a famous 7th-century warrior from the Banu Abs tribe. After his prominence, his progeny named themselves after him, becoming known as the Rashaida or children of Rasheed.

data.history:
Banu Assad (Arabic: بَنُو أَسَدْ) is an Arab tribe descended from Asad ibn Khuzayma ibn Mudrika ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, making them Adnanite Arabs and cousins of the Prophet Muhammad through their common ancestor Khuzayma ibn Mudrikah. Originally based in central Arabia's Najd region, particularly around the Aja and Salma mountains (later known as Jabal Tayy), the Banu Assad were displaced in the 2nd century CE when the Tayy tribe migrated from Yemen and took over these territories. Despite losing some territory, the two tribes ultimately became allies and intermarried.

In the 6th century, Banu Assad revolted against the Kingdom of Kindah, killing King Hujr (father of the famous poet Imru' al-Qais), which led to a prolonged war that ultimately resulted in the end of the Kindah kingdom. The tribe also formed a confederation with the Tayy and later the Ghaṭafān, though this alliance collapsed when Asad and the Ghaṭafān assaulted both branches of the Tayy and drove them from their territories in Jabal Tayy. However, one Asad leader, Dhu al-Khimarayn Awf al-Jadhami, defected and reestablished the alliance with the Tayy, together restoring their territories.

The tribe produced several prominent figures in early Islamic history, including the famous female warrior Khawla bint al-Azwar, daughter of Azwar al Asadi, one of the chiefs of Banu Assad. She fought alongside her brother Dhiraar in the Muslim conquest of the Levant, participating in decisive battles including Yarmouk in 636 against the Byzantine Empire. The tribe migrated to Iraq in the 7th century, settling near Kufa and along the Euphrates river, where they played a crucial role in early Islamic conflicts.

Banu Assad sided with Ali ibn Abi Talib in the Battle of the Camel and later supported Husayn ibn Ali at the Battle of Karbala in 680 CE, where many tribal members died as martyrs including commanders Habib ibn Muzahir and Muslim ibn Awsaja al-Asadi. After the massacre, Banu Assad members had the honor of burying Husayn and his companions with the help of Ali ibn Husayn Zayn al-Abidin. In 998, Ali ben Mazyad established the virtually independent Mazyadid emirate in the Kufa area, which lasted for a century and a half and founded the city of Hillah in 1101. The tribe is highly respected by Shia Muslims for their role at Karbala, and today members live primarily in Iraqi cities including Basra, Najaf, Kufa, Karbala, and Baghdad, with branches also found in Sudan (Banu Kahil), Iran's Khuzestan province, and historically in various other regions.

data.history:
Banu Lahb is a tribal branch within the larger Ghamid confederation, part of the ancient Azd tribal system tracing their Qahtani lineage back to pre-Islamic times. According to genealogical sources, they are among the closest tribes in lineage to Ghamid, alongside Zahran and Thamalah, all forming part of Azd Shanu'ah - the descendants of Malik ibn Nasr ibn Azd ibn Al-Ghawth ibn Nabt ibn Malik ibn Zayd ibn Kahlan ibn Saba' ibn Yashjub ibn Ya'rub ibn Qahtan. The Azd Shanu'ah tribes, including Banu Lahb's confederation, were historically noted for their distinctive physical characteristics, described as having light, muscular builds and well-proportioned frames, with Prophet Muhammad reportedly comparing Moses to the men of Shanu'ah in terms of their notable height and strength.

As part of the Ghamid tribal system, Banu Lahb's historical territory spans the southwestern regions of the Arabian Peninsula, particularly in the Al-Bahah region of the Hejaz, an area that was part of Arabia Felix which enjoyed more rainfall and productive fields than other parts of Arabia. The broader Ghamid confederation, of which Banu Lahb forms a part, is considered one of the oldest tribes of the Arabian Peninsula and has maintained continuous presence in their ancestral homeland since pre-Islamic times. While members of the confederation have spread to various countries including Iraq, Jordan, Oman, Sudan, Egypt, UAE, and Yemen, the core tribal areas remain in their southwestern Arabian homeland.

The Ghamid confederation, including Banu Lahb, participated actively in early Islamic history, with two recorded delegations to Prophet Muhammad - one in Mecca and another in Madinah in the 10th year after Hijrah. Throughout the centuries, the confederation played significant roles in regional conflicts, including confronting Abraha the Abyssinian's army when he marched toward Mecca, and later participating in various battles alongside the Sharifs of Mecca against Ottoman forces in the 18th and 19th centuries. The tribe maintains traditional social structures within the modern Saudi state, continuing to exist as part of the established Ghamid confederation that has preserved its identity and territorial presence for over a millennium.

folkLegends.title: The Naming of Ghamid
folkLegends.story: According to Ibn al-Kalbi, the ancestor Ghamid (Amr ibn Ka'b) was named Ghamid because he covered up a matter that occurred between him and his clan, so the tribal leader of Hadur called him Ghamid. However, Abu Hatim al-Sijistani and al-Asma'i rejected this view, stating that the derivation comes from 'Ghamadtu al-bi'r Ghamdan' (I covered the well), meaning the water in it became abundant.

data.nameEtymology: The name derives from their inclusion within the Ghamid confederation. The name Ghamid itself has disputed etymology - either from covering up a tribal matter or from the phrase 'Ghamadtu al-bi'r Ghamdan' meaning to make a well abundant with water by covering it.

data.history:
Banu Qays ibn Tha'laba represents a tribal branch within the broader Qays 'Aylan confederation, one of the major tribal groupings of pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia. The research reveals that within the Qays confederation structure, the Banu Sulaym tribe - descended from Khasafa through Mansur ibn Ikrima ibn Khasafa - was divided into three main divisions: Imru' al-Qays, Harith, and Tha'laba. This Tha'laba division of Banu Sulaym appears to represent the genealogical connection to the tribal ancestor referenced as Qays ibn Tha'laba, placing this tribal branch firmly within the Adnani genealogical framework through the Mudar-Qays lineage.

The Qays 'Aylan confederation, to which Banu Qays ibn Tha'laba belonged, originally inhabited the low-lying areas of the Tihamah along the Hejaz in western Arabia. By Muhammad's time around 570 CE, the numerous Qays branches had spread throughout central and eastern Arabia, including the Najd, Yamama, Bahrayn, and parts of Lower Mesopotamia. During the early Islamic period, Qaysi tribes initially opposed Muhammad but gradually converted to Islam by 630 CE, though many apostatized during the Ridda Wars before returning to the Muslim fold.

Following the Muslim conquests, massive Qaysi migrations occurred northward into Syria and Mesopotamia. During the Umayyad Caliphate (661-750), the Qays became one of the main political and military factions, often in rivalry with the Yamani tribes led by Banu Kalb. The Sulaym tribe, which included the Tha'laba division connected to Banu Qays ibn Tha'laba, participated in these major political developments and migrations. Qaysi tribes established themselves particularly in Jund Qinnasrin (northern Syria and Upper Mesopotamia), from where they governed on behalf of the caliphs or rebelled against them.

By the 14th century, only remnants of Qaysi tribes remained in their central Arabian ancestral lands, as huge numbers had migrated to North Africa in successive waves. The Banu Sulaym, the parent tribe containing the Tha'laba division, established themselves in Ifriqiya (central North Africa) and Fes. The power of the Qays as a unified confederation diminished with the rise of the Abbasid Caliphate, though individual Qaysi tribes remained potent forces throughout the Islamic world.

folkLegends.title: Origin of the Aylan Epithet
folkLegends.story: Traditional Arab genealogy holds multiple theories for the origins of the 'Aylan' part of Qays 'Aylan's name. Some genealogists claim Aylan was the name of al-Nas's (Qays's) famous horse, while others assert it was his dog, his bow, a mountain where he was born, or a man who raised him.

data.history:
Banu Tha'labah was a pre-Islamic Arabian tribe of Adnanite lineage that held significant political prominence within the Mudar tribal confederation during the 5th-6th centuries CE. According to genealogical traditions, they descended from Tha'labah ibn Dhubyān ibn Baghīd ibn Rayth ibn Ghaṭafān ibn Saʾd ibn Qays ʿAylān ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, placing them among the northern Arabian tribes. Historical inscriptions from the Himyarite kingdom indicate that Banu Tha'labah rose to become one of the chief ruling tribes over the Mudar confederation, succeeding the Salihids in this leadership role during the early 6th century. A crucial Himyarite inscription (Maʾsal 2) dated to June 521 CE specifically mentions Banu Tha'labah as leaders of Mudar forces who provided military reinforcements to a Himyarite expedition into Sasanian territory, demonstrating their significant political and military influence in central and northern Arabia at this time.

The tribe's territories were primarily located in the northern Arabian Peninsula, where they operated as part of the broader Ghatafan tribal confederation. During the pre-Islamic period, they were adherents of Christianity and maintained complex relationships with the southern Arabian powers, particularly the Himyarite kingdom, whose rulers exercised varying degrees of hegemony over the northern tribes. The Mudar confederation, including Banu Tha'labah, was frequently under the suzerainty of southern Arabian kingdoms, especially during periods when these powers held the title 'king of Maʿadd' or 'king of Mudar and Rabīʿa.'

With the rise of Islam in the early 7th century, Banu Tha'labah came into direct military conflict with Prophet Muhammad and the early Muslim community in Medina. These confrontations began with the Invasion of Thi Amr and escalated through three major raids in 627 CE. The First Raid on Banu Thalabah resulted in the deaths of nine Muslims, prompting Muhammad to order retaliatory Second and Third Raids. The Third Raid on Banu Thalabah, conducted in September 627 CE (Jumada Ath-Thania, 6 AH), was led by Zaid ibn Haritha with 15 men who successfully captured 20 camels from the tribe, though the tribe members had fled before the raid.

Following Muhammad's death and during the Ridda Wars under Caliph Abu Bakr, Banu Tha'labah eventually converted to Islam and sought integration into the Islamic state. When tribal representatives approached Abu Bakr requesting access to their traditional lands, the Caliph disputed their territorial claims but granted them pastoral rights to the al-Rabadhah area. This settlement marked the end of their independent political existence as they gradually merged into the broader Islamic community structure, losing their distinct tribal identity through integration with other groups and settlement into sedentary communities across the Arabian Peninsula.

data.history: The Caucones were an autochthonous tribe of Anatolia (modern-day Turkey) with possible origins in the Caucasus Mountains, as suggested by the phonology of their name. They are potentially related to the Late Bronze Age Kaskians who inhabited the southern shore of the Black Sea according to Hittite tablets. According to Strabo, Caucones once inhabited the southern Black Sea coast from Heraclea Pontica (modern Karadeniz Ereğli) to Carambis promontory at Teion, on the Parthenios River. According to Herodotus and other classical writers, the Caucones were among the tribes displaced or absorbed by the Bithynians, who had migrated from Thrace. In Homer's Iliad, the Caucones are listed among the Trojan allies, with their homeland described as 'towards the sea' alongside the Carians, Paionians, Leleges, and Pelasgians. A subgroup of Caucones migrated to mainland Greece, where they inhabited Lepreion in Triphylian Pylos and had custody over Hades-Demeter shrines at Mt. Minthe. According to Herodotus, these mainland Caucones were eventually expelled and dispersed to Athens and Ionian Miletos, contributing to the spread of Eleusinian religious practices throughout Greece and Asia Minor. The Milesian Caucones possessed ancestry from Pylian Codrus, son of Melanthos, according to Herodotus. By the Classical period, they had become integrated into the broader Greek cultural sphere, with some groups maintaining distinct religious and cultural practices while others were fully assimilated into Athenian and Ionian Greek identities.

folkLegends.story: Efforts were made to 'historicize' Kaukon as the early ancestor of the Athenian genos Lykomidai around 480 BC by inventing a grandson of an earth-born Phlyus named Kaukon who taught the Eleusinian Mysteries to a royal queen Messene.

data.nameEtymology: The phonology of the name Caucones has been taken as evidence for an origin in the Caucasus Mountains
